FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, longitudinal baseline 2016-2018 (ages 9-10) to Year 2 follow-up, N=10,317

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone as exposure; no separately-measured qualifying smartphone/SM variable with own coefficient. B3 applies.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 not met; cyberbullying alone does not qualify per B3.
notes: Paper reports associations between ACEs (predictor) and cyberbullying victimization (outcome), but cyberbullying is not a qualifying SM/smartphone variable per B3. Study excluded at FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study Year 2, cross-sectional (2018-2020), N=9,443 early adolescents (10-14 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization/perpetration (outcome), adjusting for screen time (covariate). No qualifying SM/smartphone as primary exposure. B3 applies—cyberbullying alone.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 not met; cyberbullying alone as exposure does not qualify per B3.
notes: Screen time included as covariate, not as primary exposure. Cyberbullying is dependent variable. Excluded at FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, cross-sectional Year 2 (2018-2020), N=10,339 early adolescents (10-14 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen/SM variables as outcomes: total recreational screen time, texting, social media, video chat, single-player gaming, multi-player gaming, video game addiction (VGAQ), social media addiction (SMAQ), mobile phone involvement (MPIQ). B1 satisfied: separate coefficients reported for SM, texting, video chat, app-category gaming.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression with sexual orientation (sexual minority, possible sexual minority vs. heterosexual) as predictor; regression β coefficients and 95% CIs reported for associations with screen time and problematic use measures.

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients with 95% confidence intervals reported in Tables 2 and 3 for all screen time outcomes and problematic use measures. Effect sizes with CIs extractable from main results tables.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD cohort; multiple linear regression examining associations between sexual minority status and screen use outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Not a preprint, duplicate, or secondary analysis of published design; published in Annals of Epidemiology 2023, peer-reviewed.

FT_EC7_reason: Sexual orientation is NOT in the demographic-only closed set. Per coder_prompt NOT EC7 clause: 'sexual orientation, gender identity' disqualifies. Also B19 applies: cross-sectional sociodemographic/identity analysis. Paper has SM as dependent variable with sociodemographic/identity predictor but does not meet FT-EC7 criteria due to non-demographic predictor.
notes: INCLUDE. Multiple qualifying SM/smartphone variables with separate coefficients. Inferential tests (regression) with extractable estimates. Original ABCD analysis. Does not meet FT-EC7 due to sexual orientation predictor (excluded from demographic-only set).
